title: Appendix C: Powers of Search - Statutory Provisions
body_html:
CCRA

The CCRA sets out the definitions of the different kinds of searches:

46. In sections 47 to 67,

"non-intrusive search" means:

(a) a search of a non-intrusive nature of the clothed body by technical means, in the prescribed manner, and
(b) a search of

(i) personal possessions, including clothing, that the person may be carrying, and
(ii) any coat or jacket that the person has been requested to remove, in accordance with any applicable regulations

"Frisk search" means

(a) a manual search, or a search by technical means, of the clothed body, in the prescribed manner, and
(b) a search of

(i) personal possessions, including clothing, that the person may be carrying, and
(ii) any coat or jacket that the person has been requested to remove, in accordance with any applicable regulations

"Strip search" means

(a) a visual inspection of the naked body, in the prescribed manner, and
(b) a search, in accordance with any applicable regulations...of all clothing, things in the clothing, and other personal possessions which the person may be carrying

"Body cavity search" means the physical probing of a body cavity, in the prescribed manner.

The CCR Regulations set out "the prescribed manner" for carrying out the various kinds of searches. Thus in relation to strip and body cavity searches, the Regulations provide:

45 A strip search shall consist of a visual inspection of the person by a staff member, in the course of which inspection the person being searched shall undress completely in front of the staff member and may be required to open the person's mouth, display the soles of their feet, run their fingers through their hair, present open hands and arms, bend over or otherwise enable a staff member to perform a visual inspection.

46 A strip search and a body cavity search shall be carried out in a private area that is out of sight of every other person except for one staff member of the same sex as the person being searched, which staff member is required to be present as a witness unless, in the case of a strip search, the search is an emergency as described in subsection 49(4) of the Act.

Section 47 of the CCRA provides for "routine" non-intrusive or frisk searches of prisoners.

47(1) A staff member may conduct routine non-intrusive searches or routine frisk searches of inmates, without individualised suspicion, in the prescribed circumstances, which circumstances must be limited to what is reasonably required for security purposes.

The CCR Regulations set out the manner of carrying out such searches and the circumstances under which they are reasonably required for security purposes.

43 A non-intrusive search shall be carried out by means of a hand-held scanner, a walk-through scanner whereby the person being searched is required to walk through a metal detector scanner, or any similar non-intrusive devise.

44 A frisk search shall be carried out from head to foot, down the front and rear of the body, around the legs and inside clothing folds, pockets and footwear.

Section 47 authorises routine non-intrusive or frisk searches where a prisoner is entering or leaving a penitentiary, the open or family visiting area, a work place or the segregation area.

Paralleling the scheme for routine non-intrusive and frisk searches, the CCRA and Regulations set out the circumstances under which routine strip searches can be conducted and the manner of carrying out such searches. Section 48 of the CCRA provides:

48 A staff member of the same sex as the inmate may conduct a routine strip search of an inmate, without individualised suspicion,

(a) in the prescribed circumstances, which circumstances must be limited to situations in which the inmate has been in a place where there was a likelihood of access to contraband that is capable of being hidden on or in the body; or
(b) when the inmate is entering or leaving a segregation area.

Section 49 of the CCRA deals with investigative searches of prisoners who are believed to be carrying contraband or evidence of an offence. This section authorizes either a frisk search or a strip search and differentiates between the two in terms of both the criteria for the search and the necessity for prior authorisation; such authorisation is only required for strip searches.

49(1) Where a staff member suspects on reasonable grounds that an inmate is carrying contraband or carrying evidence relating to a disciplinary or criminal offence, the staff member may conduct a frisk search of the inmate.

(3) Where a staff member

(a) believes on reasonable grounds that an inmate is carrying contraband or carrying evidence relating to a disciplinary or criminal offence, and that a strip search is necessary to find the contraband or evidence, and
(b) satisfies the institutional head that there are reasonable grounds to so believe, a staff member of the same sex as the inmate may conduct a strip search of the inmate.

Section 52 of the CCRA provides separately for body cavity searches for investigative purposes:

52 Where the institutional head is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that an inmate is carrying contraband in a body cavity and that a body cavity search is necessary in order to find or seize the contraband, the institutional head may authorize in writing a body cavity search to be conducted by a qualified medical practitioner, if the inmate's consent is obtained.

In addition to "routine" and "investigative" searches the CCRA also authorizes "emergency" searches. Section 49(4) provides:

49(4) Where a staff member

(a) satisfies the requirements of paragraph (3)(a), and
(b) believes on reasonable grounds that the delay that would be necessary in order to comply with paragraph (3)(b) or with the gender requirement of subsection (3) would result in danger to human life or safety or in loss or destruction of the evidence, the staff member may conduct the strip search without complying with paragraph (3)(b) [i.e. without obtaining the authorization of the institutional head] or the gender requirement of subsection (3) [i.e. a staff member of a different gender may conduct the search].

Related to the provisions for emergency searches are what are termed "exceptional powers of search". They include either frisk or strip searching of all inmates in the penitentiary or a part thereof. Section 53 provides:

53(1) Where the institutional head is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that

(a) there exists, because of contraband, a clear and substantial danger to human life or safety or to the security of the penitentiary, and
(b) a frisk search or strip search of all the inmates in the penitentiary or any part thereof is necessary in order to seize the contraband and avert the danger, the institutional head may authorize in writing such a search, subject to subjection (2).

(2) A strip search authorized under subsection (1) shall be conducted in each case by a staff member of the same sex as the inmate.

The CCRA and Regulations also address the issue of searches of cells and draw the same distinction between routine, investigative and emergency searches (s. 58 CCRA; ss. 51-3 CCR Regulations). In keeping with the recommendations of the Correctional Law Reform the CCR Regulations further provide detailed provisions regarding post-search reports (s. 58 CCR Regulations). The Commissioner's Directive on Searchcomplements the provisions of the CCRA and Regulations by providing that the policy objectives of the searching of prisoners are:

1. To establish and define the requirements and procedures for searching inmates in institutions and community correctional centres.

2. To search inmates in order to prevent the possession of contraband and unauthorized items while having due regard for privacy and for the dignity of the individual being searched. (C.D. 566-7, December 17, 2001)
